docs.title: Understanding Logic WiP coded in TPTP THF and TFF
Understanding Logic WiP coded in TPTP THF and TFF
Setup
docs.text:
Create a src directory to use as your TPTP HOME and set the TPTP environment variable with the absolute path: 

mkdir src && cd src
export TPTP=$(pwd)
git clone git@github.com:CoreSenseEU/understanding-logic.git
This results in include() paths being read relative to the src directory. Other TPTP files can be placed in the same src directory matching this tree structure: 
src
├── my_package
│   ├── example1.tff
│   ├── example2.thf
│   └── ...
└── understanding-logic
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── README.md
    ├── tff
    │   └── ...
    └── thf
        └── ...

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
The documentation is available at https://coresenseeu.github.io/understanding-logic/.

The documentation is built using the Material for MkDocs framework, which allows for easy documentation generation from markdown files. While it is based on the CommonMark flavor of Markdown, many extensions are supported which add extra features such as Admonitions, Content Tabs, and Math to name a few.

To build it locally, see the Documentation Instructions.

docs.title: Development Notes
docs.text:
Because Higher Order Logic (HOL) is not currently well supported in Vampire, any .thf files need to obey a few rules to avoid problems with their TPTP parser. - Do not use builtin predicates or functiors ($distinct, $let, $ite). - Do not use builtin theory axioms ($less, $greatereq, $floor, etc.). - Do not use cannonical tff or fol formulas. You can convert them all to thf by rewriting functions and predicates with the @ operator between arguments. For example: 

tff(my_formula, axiom, my_function(v1, v2, v3) = v4).

% -->
thf(my_formula, axiom, ((my_function @ v1 @ v2 @ v3) = v4)).

docs.title: Documentation Instructions
docs.text: This repository contains the source and configuration files used to generate documentation for the CoreSense Understanding System.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
This website is built using the Material for MkDocs framework, which allows for easy documentation generation from markdown files. While it is based on the CommonMark flavor of Markdown, many extensions are supported which add extra features such as Admonitions, Content Tabs, and Math to name a few.

Simply install Material for MkDocs (and a few plugins) with pip, ideally within a virtual environment. 

pip install -r docs/requirements.txt

docs.title: Live preview
docs.text:
MkDocs (which Material for MkDocs is built on) provides a live preview server at localhost:8000 that will automatically rebuild whenever you save a source file. It can be started with: 

mkdocs serve

docs.title: Build instructions
docs.text:
Change to the root directory of this repository then build the static site in the build directory using the -d flag (default build directory is site): 

mkdocs build -d build

docs.title: Modifying documentation
docs.text:
The best way to modify the documentation is by directly editing the Markdown source files in the docs directory in your favorite text editor. However, for a quick fix you can click the icon at the top of the page you wish to edit and it will take you to the Github live editor window.

Info

In this repository, quick fix edits will be make in the documentation branch. You will need to manually merge the changes into rolling according to the project Contribution Guidelines.

TODO

Create Contribution Guidelines in contributing.md.

If there is an extension or plugin you would like to add for additional functionality, feel free to edit the mkdocs.yml file.

docs.title: Adding new pages
docs.text: If you want to add a whole new page to the documentation you can create a new markdown .md file in the docs directory, or the appropriate sub-directory if the page is part of a section.

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text:
Danger

These concepts are still in development and high susceptible to change. Use the provided terms, definitions, and types with caution.

The Understanding System (a.k.a Understanding Core) is a CoreSense module for generating understanding within a CoreSense agent. The main idea is to:

Intuition

Find strategies of Engine exertions to create required Modelets.

TODO

Add a map of the concepts or something more elegant here... We should also include the triangle of meaning.

docs.title: agent
docs.text: A CoreSense agent which may posess engines and modelets.

docs.title: datatype
docs.text: A precise information encoding that is taken as granted.

docs.title: engine
docs.text: Does work within a CoreSense agent to produce modelets.

docs.title: exertion
docs.text: Check if a modelet matches a template.

docs.title: extent
docs.text: A 4-dimensional size (3 space, 1 time).

docs.title: formalism
docs.text: The language and structure in which a phenomenon could be described.

docs.title: modelet
docs.text: Models a phenomenon as the thought in the triangle of meaning.

docs.title: modelet_set
docs.text: Some particular set of modelets which the has reasoner assembled.

docs.title: origin
docs.text: An agent's perspective on the phenomenon captured in a particular modelet.

docs.title: phenomenon
docs.text: Some space or object or being or process in the real world that the system represents using modelets.

docs.title: property
docs.text: A specific characteristic of a modelet or engine.

docs.title: requirement
docs.text: A restriction on the property values that a template may enforce on a modelet.

docs.title: representation_class
docs.text: The purpose of a modelet (extrinsic to itself) when used by an engine.

docs.title: spacetime_point
docs.text: 4-dimensional coordinates (3 space, 1 time).

docs.title: strategy
docs.text: A succession of exertions of engines connects modelets.

docs.title: template
docs.text: Describes the features that a single modelet should have for an engine to accept it as input.

docs.title: template_set
docs.text: Some particular set of templates which describe the inputs to an engine.

docs.title: concept
docs.text: An instance of a class of phenomenon in the agent's world model.

docs.title: agent $tType
docs.text:
A CoreSense agent which may posess engines and modelets.

Unimplemented

To be introduced later, as collective awareness is considered.

docs.title: concept $tType
docs.text:
An instance of a class of phenomenon in the agent's world model.

A concept is a property of a modelet.

Example

  • chair
  • dog
  • the color pink
  • sunrise

docs.title: datatype $tType
docs.text:
A precise information encoding that is taken as granted. As the name suggests, the fundamental types of data considered.

Example

  • real number
  • uint8
  • pixel

docs.title: ROS 2 Interface builtins
docs.text:
ROS 2 interfaces (messages, services, and actions) are formalisms constructed from semantically named builtin datatypes and arrays of these builtins. See the ROS 2 documentation for more details.

Important

These individuals are not associated with a particular semantic label. The intention (WIP) is to match a particular datatype (the fieldtype) and a concept (the fieldname) in some formalism representing the ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Bool' datatype
docs.text: The builtin bool field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Byte' datatype
docs.text: The builtin byte field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Char' datatype
docs.text: The builtin char field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Float32' datatype
docs.text: The builtin float32 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Float64' datatype
docs.text: The builtin float64 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Int8' datatype
docs.text: The builtin int8 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Uint8' datatype
docs.text: The builtin uint8 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Int16' datatype
docs.text: The builtin int16 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Uint16' datatype
docs.text: The builtin uin16 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Int32' datatype
docs.text: The builtin int32 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Uint32' datatype
docs.text: The builtin uint32 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Int64' datatype
docs.text: The builtin int64 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Uint64' datatype
docs.text: The builtin uint64 field type of a ROS 2 interface.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.String' datatype
docs.text: The builtin (unbounded) string field type of a ROS 2 interface. These strings use 8-bit characters.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.Wstring' datatype
docs.text: The builtin wstring field type of a ROS 2 interface. These strings use 16-bit characters.

docs.title: ROS 2 Interface arrays
docs.text:
ROS 2 interfaces support arrays of builtins, optionally with a fixed or bounded size.

Warning

Sizes of array datatypes are assumed to be natural numbers. This is NOT enforced by the type checker or logic.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.BoundedString' ($int>datatype)
docs.text: A bounded string<=n field type of a ROS 2 interface. n is a natural number.

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.UnboundedDynamicArray' (datatype>datatype)
docs.text:
An array of any builtin with an unbouned size field type in a ROS 2 interface.

Example

  • char[]
  • float32[]

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.BoundedDynamicArray' ((datatype*$int) >datatype)
docs.text:
A dynamic array of any builtin with a bounded size field type in a ROS 2 interface. The size must be a natural number.

Example

  • char[<=5]
  • float32[<=4]

docs.title: 'ROS2.msg.StaticArray' ((datatype*$int) >datatype)
docs.text:
A static array of any builtin with a fixed size field type in a ROS 2 interface. The size must be a natural number.

Example

  • char[5]
  • float32[4]

docs.title: engine $tType
docs.text:
Does work within a CoreSense agent to produce modelets.

  • Takes multiple modelets as an input modelet_set
  • Uses semantic information to identify valid input modelet formalisms
    • semantics could be external (part of the type definition) or internal (modeled within the modelet itself)
  • Produces one output modelet
    • May or may not impart semantic meaning to the output modelet
    • May (re)define the output modelet origin, concept, or characteristics
  • Has exertion characteristics
    • e.g. time delay, resource usage, power consumption

Example

  • DroneStateEngine (see tff/tests/test-wind-estimation.tff)
    • Input Model:
      • Modelet
        • formalism: IMU.msg
        • modelled concept: inertia
      • Modelet
        • formalism: NavSatFix.msg
        • modelled concept: geolocation
    • Output Modelet:
      • modelet
        • formalism: DroneState.msg
        • modelled concept: drone_state

docs.title: interface_of (engine>template_set)
docs.text: An engine requires a set of input modelets which matches the template_set.

docs.title: output_modelet_formalism (engine>formalism)
docs.text: The formalism of an engine output modelet.

docs.title: is_output_modelet_concept (engine>phenomenon)
docs.text: The concept of an engine output modelet.

docs.title: output_property_of_engine (engine>property)
docs.text: The property an engine imparts on its output modelet.

docs.title: engine_imparts_representation_class (engine>representation_class)
docs.text: The representation_class an engine imparts on its output modelet.

docs.title: inputs_match ((modelet*template) >*$o)
docs.text:
Check if a modelet matches a template.

  • Check if they are formally equivalent
  • Check if the modelet contains permissible values for each of the properties of requirements of the template.

docs.title: formally_equivalent ((template*modelet) >$o)
docs.text: A template and a modelet share the same phenomenon and formalism.

docs.title: interfaces_match ((modelet_set*template_set) >$o)
docs.text:
Check if all of the templates in the template_set have a corresponding modelet in the modelet_set with matching profile.

  • Check if they are formally equivalent
  • Check if their inputs match

docs.title: exert ((engine*modelet_set*modelet) >$o)
docs.text: Exert an engine on a modelet_set to produce an output modelet.

docs.title: exertable ((modelet_set*engine) >$o)
docs.text: A modelet_set can be exerted by an engine iff their interfaces match.

docs.title: extent $tType
docs.text:
A 4-dimensional size (3 space, 1 time).

Note

Not to be confused with a 4-dimentional spacetime_point.

docs.title: extent_width (extent>$real)
docs.text: The width of an (extent).

docs.title: extent_height (extent>$real)
docs.text: The height of an (extent).

docs.title: extent_depth (extent>$real)
docs.text: The depth of an (extent).

docs.title: extent_duration (extent>$real)
docs.text: The duration of an (extent).

docs.title: formalism $tType
docs.text:
The language and structure in which a phenomenon could be described. Methods used to express modelets; class in the Data Model.

  • The information structure used to capture the concept and origin
  • Highlights some aspects of the phenomenon while hiding others
  • Defines engine I/O compatibility and usability with other models

New formalism could be defined or old ones modified.

Example

  • ROS message
  • Image format
  • Language grammar

docs.title: modelet $tType
docs.text:
Models a phenomenon as the thought in the triangle of meaning. All modelets describe an instance of a concept, have an origin, and are modeled in a specific formalism.

  • Concept: the phenomenon of the modelet, an instance of a class in the world model.
  • Origin: the agent's perspective on the phenomenon
  • Formalism: the representation of the phenomenon

docs.title: modelet_set $tType
docs.text:
Some particular set of modelets which the has reasoner assembled. Used to collect the inputs to an engine.

Example

  • {m1, m2, m3}
  • {m2}
  • {}

docs.title: modelet_models_concept (modelet>phenomenon)
docs.text:
The phenomenon symbolizing the phenomenon of a modelet.

TODO

rename from 'concept' to something else.

docs.title: formalism_of_modelet (modelet>formalism)
docs.text: The formalism used by a modelet to describe the phenomenon.

docs.title: modelet_creation_date (modelet>$real)
docs.text:
The date a modelet was created.

TODO

The date is represented as a $real but we don't make any assumptions about what that number represents. Unix time? time since last restart? seconds? This may be solved by integration with spacetime_point.

docs.title: modelet_has_location (modelet>spacetime_point)
docs.text: The time and spatial location a modelet refers to. Modelets may have a specific temporal scope or make an observation at a specific spacetime_point.

docs.title: modelet_has_extent (modelet>extent)
docs.text: The time and spatial extent a modelet refers to. Modelets may have an area/volume/duration of reference.

docs.title: modelet_has_representation_class (modelet>representation_class)
docs.text:
The representation_class a modelet's relation to its parents has after being created by an engine.

TODO

This looks like it will turn out to be HOL.

docs.title: modelet_has_creator (modelet>engine)
docs.text: The engine that created the modelet.

docs.title: is_in_modelet_set ((modelet*modelet_set) >$o)
docs.text: Check if a modelet is a member of a modelet_set.

docs.title: origin $tType
docs.text:
An agent's perspective on the phenomenon captured in a particular modelet.

  • Observation is situated in time and space
  • Measured with sensors that have certain characteristics and limitations
    • (inferred) physical quality (e.g. distance inferred from delay)
    • resolution and frame rate
    • reflections and transparencies could be misinterpreted

docs.title: phenomenon $tType
docs.text:
Some space or object or being or process in the real world that the system represents using modelets.

  • Situated in time and space
  • May change over time
  • Associated with a mission-dependent meaning

Example

  • you
  • your office
  • your tea
  • your tea approaching room temperature

docs.title: property $tType
docs.text:
A specific characteristic of a modelet or engine. Multiple things can exhibit the same property.

Example

  • color: red
  • age: 14 years

docs.title: has_value (property>$real)
docs.text: The value of a certain property.

docs.title: datatype_of_property (property>datatype)
docs.text: The datatype of a certain property.

docs.title: is_property_of_modelet ((property*modelet) >$o)
docs.text: Check if a modelet is described by a certain property.

docs.title: is_property_of_engine ((property*engine) >$o)
docs.text: Check if an engine is described by a certain property.

docs.title: representation_class $tType
docs.text: The purpose of a modelet (extrinsic to itself) when used by an engine. Class in the Processing/Modelling Model.

docs.title: requirement $tType
docs.text: A restriction on the property values that a template may enforce on a modelet.

docs.title: datatype_of_requirement (requirement>datatype)
docs.text: The datatype of a certain requirement.

docs.title: is_permissible ((requirement*$real) >$o)
docs.text: Check if a value is permissible under a certain requirement.

docs.title: is_part_of ((requirement*template) >$o)
docs.text: Check if a requirement is part of a template.

docs.title: spacetime_point $tType
docs.text:
4-dimensional coordinates (3 space, 1 time).

Note

Not to be confused with a 4-dimentional extent.

docs.title: point_x (spacetime_point>$real)
docs.text: The spatial x coordinate of a point in space and time (spacetime_point).

docs.title: point_y (spacetime_point>$real)
docs.text: The spatial y coordinate of a point in space and time (spacetime_point).

docs.title: point_z (spacetime_point>$real)
docs.text: The spatial z coordinate of a point in space and time (spacetime_point).

docs.title: point_t (spacetime_point>$real)
docs.text: The time t coordinate of a point in space and time (spacetime_point).

docs.title: strategy ((modelet_set*modelet) >$o)
docs.text:
A succession of exertions of engines connects modelets. Strategies are transitive.

  • Must respect engine requirements
  • Matches modelet phenomenon semantics to physical reality
    • e.g. "on the living room table" to coordinates

docs.title: template $tType
docs.text:
Describes the features that a single modelet should have for an engine to accept it as input.

  • a semantic function signature
  • properties and requirements of modelets

docs.title: template_set $tType
docs.text:
Some particular set of templates which describe the inputs to an engine.

Example

  • {t1, t2, t3}
  • {t2}
  • {}

docs.title: template_has_concept_requirement (template>concept)
docs.text: The concept symbolizing the phenomenon class of a template.

docs.title: template_formalism_requirement (template>formalism)
docs.text: The formalism this template expects the phenomenon to be described by.

docs.title: template_has_location_requirement (template>spacetime_point)
docs.text: The time and spatial location a template refers to. A template may have a specific temporal scope or expect an observation at a specific spacetime_point.

docs.title: template_has_extent_requirement (template>extent)
docs.text: The time and spatial extent a template covers. A template may expect an area/volume/duration of reference.

docs.title: template_has_creator_requirement (template>engine)
docs.text: The engine a template requires a modelet to have been created by.

docs.title: is_in_template_set ((template*template_set) >$o)
docs.text: Check if a template is a member of a template_set.

docs.title: Usage and Installation
docs.text:
The TFF models and tests can be run using the latest release or master branch of the Vampire theorem prover.

If you want to run the THF models, you need to either install the LEO-III theorem prover or build the Vampire hol branch from source using these instructions. Make sure to add the -DCMAKE_BUILD_HOL=ON flag.
